column7:
You're describing a cycle of abuse. When you express your pain, you do so as a weapon; to hurt others so that they will change.

Ultimately, that just continues the cycle of abuse.

The divisiveness strategy was ultimately *unsuccessful* in this election, but that doesn't mean it was counterproductive.  

It's only counterproductive if it results in a net loss in votes.  Sure, their tactics pissed off some moderates, but they did manage to fire up their base.  You have to ask yourself, if the Democrats didn't use those divisive tactics, how much of their base constituency would show up for elections?  It's likely they would've had less turnout, possibly more than offsetting the Trump supporters who were motivated by the hate.

And make no mistake, it's a deliberate strategy.  See the leaked Podesta memos and emails.  The theory is that as the demographic profile of the U.S. shifts, Democrats will be increasingly successful if they can keep blacks, latinos, and LGBT locked down.  It didn't pan out for them this time, but how else are you supposed to maximize turnout if you rely on marginalized groups as your base?

I'm not sure if I should be flattered or scared by the idea of Democrats trying to lock down marginalized groups to maximize turnout. Sounds like a pretty manipulative strategy to me. But hey, if it works, I guess they'll keep doing it, right? 😅

column7: So I have a bit of a different perspective on this, I’m an African American male who works in a STEM related field, and we have A LOT of programs and marketing material related to trying to increase the number of women in our “male dominated field”. However, we have in comparison functionally zero programs related to helping men of color. I feel as though if you’re coming from a systemic oppression angle, men of color have historically had it just as bad if not worse in some respects than white women, so I definitely see something wrong there. If you’re going try to correct historical prejudices in the workplace, I feel as though you ought to at least attempt to correct most of them equally, or else it legitimately is simple favoritism.

column7:
>[...] people will use those actions with their own private intentions, and you can't legislate that. 

Right, and private intentions aren't what I'm talking about. We agree that **people** use laws as they are, regardless of the intentions behind them. When passing **new laws**, however, (like one that would allow financial abortion), the intent of whatever you cite *does* matter. To phrase it another way: the government wouldn't be inconsistent by passing safe haven laws and denying financial abortion laws. 

>People acting within the boundaries of the law are not abusing it.

I never said abusing the law, I said abusing the system, as synonymous with [gaming the system](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gaming_the_system). Not really an important distinction, anyways. We'll assume I said private individuals. 

>Yes, and? So would the local employment chances in the area, the attitude of her employer towards pregnancy, the general economic climate, the quality of the local schools, her housing situation, attitude of her parents and community, etc. etc. There are all facts of life people factor in in their decisions.

Financial abortion would affect the decision-making process *while* the pregnancy is underway, and would be facilitated by the law -- not by life or unfortunate happenstance (housefire, flood, etc). The factors you mention are observable before pregnancy, so they would be more aptly associated with the decision to have sex in the first place. 

>There are plenty of people who don't have children because they don't have a partner. They don't get to coerce someone else into supporting their ambitions either.

Because those "someone else"s never consented to intercourse. A father isn't some passerby who happened to get lassoed into child support. He had consensual sex and assumed the risk of a child being born. When one *is* born, the priority becomes the wellbeing of that child. 

Should the state foot the bill for the kid? Not really... Only if deemed absolutely necessary, because the parents were responsible for the pregnancy. (Both of them.)

Should a mother alone foot the bill for the kid? No, because creating that situation also creates a financial coercion into abortion -- a medical procedure which *should be* entirely at the discretion of the mother. The other potential outcome is inadequate support for the child (edit: in which case, the state might need to be involved anyways).

>That's correct, because she has the pregnancy which is technically inseperable from whether there will be a child or not.

We wouldn't say that children are caused by mothers not having abortions, we'd say that they're caused by two individuals' mutual decisions to have sex. It doesn't seem right to fault someone for inaction on a supposedly optional medical procedure, especially not one with potentially profound emotional/moral/health significance. (Note: I'm not saying abortion isn't statistically safer than childbirth. Individual cases matter here, though, and we can't be privy to those.)

>So then that is an inequality: women get a second chance at protection while men don't. That should be rectified.  

There's an intrinsic imbalance because of biology. It's not fair, sure, but until a suitable compromise is reached, the carrier of the child is going to have the most options. Either way, the existence of abortion is not in-any-way a snub to the man's rights. He has the same rights as he would if abortion were not an option. 

>Not having a child does not cost anyone anything.

The child still exists under financial abortion.

I'm going to take a different direction with the argument, one that is usually in these threads but I don't see at the moment. 

There is no inequality. Men and women have the _exact_ same choices regarding raising a child. These choices are thus:

1. Have sex, knowing that it can possibly result in a child. Once that child is born, both men and women have to pay for it.
2. Have your body sustain the fetus. Since everyone has a right to bodily autonomy, both the woman and the men can decide if they want to use their body to sustain the fetus. Since women usually carry this burden, this choice comes up virtually only for women, but if a man had to hook up his kidneys to the fetus, he'd have the choice to not do that. 

Morally and legally, there is no inequality. The "inequality" arises from biology. Complaining about that is the same as complaining about why men get to choose to have a circumcision and women don't. 

The situation you're proposing doesn't make things far more fair. In fact it makes things _far_ more unequal. What it does is give the man an extra choice, one the woman doesn't have. 

The alternative and fair proposal would be this: both the man and woman can decide to have the kid and then absolve themselves from financial responsibility. The kid is then raised by the state, i.e. tax dollars. I do not have a problem with that, but some people will. However, any argument for relieving men from child support _neccesarily_ has to include women having the option too in order to be fair. The choice to have an abortion is fundamentally different from the choice of having no financial responsibility for the kid.

I think there's a fundamental misunderstanding here. The idea that men and women have the exact same choices regarding raising a child is flawed because of the biological reality of pregnancy. Women carry the physical burden of pregnancy and childbirth, which is a significant factor in the decision-making process. While men can choose not to participate in the pregnancy, they cannot choose not to be a parent. This isn't about giving men an extra choice; it's about recognizing the inherent inequality in the current system.

Moreover, the argument that not having a child doesn't cost anyone anything is also flawed. Financial abortion doesn't eliminate the child; it just shifts the financial burden to the state. This isn't a fair solution when the decision to terminate a pregnancy is often made under immense pressure and stress.

We need to find a balance where both parents share the responsibilities and the costs fairly, without forcing women to make impossible choices.

column7:
>An indigenous peoples day would be widely mocked, partly because the name is just too much, and of course because it's a super pretentious way to pretend to care about the difficulties of American Indians but do nothing significant about it. It would also cause completely unnecessary controversy and upset by replacing another holiday. The day would basically become not a holiday, but a day of political grandstanding.

This is an argument I can definitely sympathize with, in the sense that renaming a holiday isn't accomplishing much in the way of embracing Native Americans into the wider U.S. culture, but I'd argue it's a necessary starting point, since the only major cultural presence of Natives right now is sports teams mascots, and that's just despicable. We need to raise awareness about Indigenous cultures and allow them to become just as culturally significant as White Americans, African Americans, and Mexican Americans.... this is the first step toward reconciliation.
column19: ['Busy', 'Democrat', 'Always seen with the latest music equipment']

column7:
> but I'd argue it's a necessary starting point, since the only major cultural presence of Natives right now is sports teams mascots, and that's just despicable

[They didn't care.](https://www.washingtonpost.com/page/2010-2019/WashingtonPost/2016/05/19/National-Politics/Polling/release_424.xml?tid=a_inl) If they don't find it offensive, and no offense is meant, what pray tell is despicable? In fact, the Red Skins are very much meant as an honoring of native americans if you look at their mascot. The mascot depicts them as much nobler than history would suggest many were. It depicts native Americans as the best among them, being noble people. When in fact many could be best described as [savages](https://www.ohio.edu/orgs/glass/vol/1/14.htm). column7:
This study basically summarized all known research into guns. The 1995 study by Kleck and Gertz has been cited in the book, and is in the part that gun advocates use to support defensive use of guns. For example:

> However, other studies conclude that gun ownership protects against serious injury when guns are used defensively (Kleck and Gertz, 1995; Tark and Kleck, 2004).

That study I've refuted above.

I can't claim to read the entire document, so I found an [article](https://www.washingtonpost.com/news/wonk/wp/2015/01/16/the-study-that-gun-rights-activists-keep-citing-but-completely-misunderstand/?utm_term=.94863b9db630) that summarized for me. 

The statistic that you have above that appears in this reports originates in the 1995 study by Kleck and Gertz. If you have any other points from this study that you want to pick out, I can look into the validity of that research. 

As it stands, the 2013 study Priorities is indeed a document designed to describe the current state of research and call for more research, not to give solutions. The title is a bit of hint to what the aims of the research was, honestly.

Federal research dollars have answered the question about seat belt safety, and now seat belts are regulated on a industrial level (i.e. that all new cars must have them and they must comply with certain specification) and on a public level (i.e. that every passenger in the car must be restrained.

This is a great model to apply to the gun debate.

column7:
>Yes but the media is unfortunately shaped by western media which is biased. Sure, many western movies and TV shows have a diverse cast, but usually it is the white people who get the main roles.

A lot of this is just marketing to an audience.  Latin American programs tend to reflect the ethnic make up of their audience.  Same goes for movies from China.  Likewise, the US is mostly white (more or less) people so it makes sense that our media would reflect the ethnic makeup of the country just like it does everywhere else. 

> This is a form of subtle racism where many POC women are conditioned by western society to see white men as more appealing than other POC men: http://www.meldmagazine.com.au/2013/11/asian-australian-online-dating-michele-lee/

This doesn't have any merit as a source.  Just seems to be one author's meanderings....

>Latin American programs tend to reflect the ethnic make up of their audience. Same goes for movies from China. 

Says the westerner who only watches western TV shows and movies. That's simply not true. So many of the most popular Latin American celebrities are white or white-passing (eg. Juanes and Shakira) and they are over represented in Latin American pop culture:
https://www.quora.com/Why-is-there-a-dominance-of-white-actors-in-Latin-American-movies-and-TV-shows

In some Latin American countries, non-white locals think of having a white spouse and having whiter kids as a way of "mejorar la raza" or improving the race: http://www.huffingtonpost.com/maria-alejandra-casalehardin-/mejorar-la-raza-an-exampl_b_7558892.html?

If it's not racism/a colonial mindset, tell me what it is.

This is the same in Asian countries. In Japan (a country where almost everyone is 100% Asian), half-white girls find it easier to find roles on TV: https://www.tofugu.com/japan/hafu-japan/

You may argue that it is exoticism, but you don't see many half-black celebrities on Japanese TV (maybe except for the previous Miss Japan). These half white celebrities are over-represented in Japan, considering that they make up less than 1% of the Japanese population.

>Same goes for movies from China.

I am from Hong Kong and I will answer this for you.

I think it has more to do with language barriers. Most white people living in China cannot speak Chinese, and even if they do, they speak it with an accent. The same applies to half-white kids growing up in China. You can get away with not knowing Chinese if you go to international schools unlike Japan where you can't get away with not knowing Japanese. Do you think Chinese people who are not fluent in English would want to watch a Chinese movie full of white actors and actresses speaking *English*?

In the entire Sinophone, only Macau has a significant white and Eurasian population which speaks Cantonese without an accent, but Macau doesn't really have its own entertainment industry. They just import other stuff from other areas in the Sinophone.

A disproportionate of celebrities in mainland China and Hong Kong have Eurasian features. Examples include Fan Bing Bing, Grace Chan and Angelababy. There's also the Hong Kong Eurasian supermodel Jessica C.

Whitish features are seen as ideal here even though we are not white. You may disagree with this but tbh I think it has more to do with how you're not familiar with Chinese culture (Asian-Americans are culturally different from Asians in Asia). If you ever come to Hong Kong, you will see that Eurasian models are over represented in fashion magazines and ads.

Read this: http://edition.cnn.com/2013/06/25/health/asian-beauty/

I've noticed that the discussion often revolves around representation in media, but let's not forget the impact of cultural context and history. For example, in many Western countries, there's a long history of colonization and cultural dominance, which might influence media representation. It's important to acknowledge these factors while also pushing for more diverse and inclusive content. Let's keep the conversation respectful and constructive!

column7: Well maybe if y'all weren't so hostile about normal shit we wouldn't have to get weird to move the bar.

>Are black people not allowed to listen to gangster rap and get rowdy with their friends, lest they be seen as scary or intimidating? Are Hispanic people not allowed to speak Spanish in public, lest they be seen as a foreign or "other" people? What about Muslims and Sikhs wearing traditional garb?

These and wearing a gimp suit on all 4s are two very different things. I'm not going to shield a kids eyes from a Muslim or Spanish speaker. Speaking Spanish or wearing religious attire is pretty normal and accepted as normal behavior. Wearing fursuits, strap ons and gimp suits isn't. 

Your saying OP is wanting people to conform to him for acceptance, but these over sexualized pride events are doing that same thing. Wanting people to conform and accept behaviors that are unnatural in society. Whether you like it or not, conformity needs to happen. All societies are based on conformity. From the ancient Egyptian civilizations to the Middle eastern societies to America. When there is no conformity its called chaos. To be apart of society, the individual must conform to society, not the other way around. 

If it were just LGBT people having a normal parade/march/party it'd be all fine and good, even if they act the way they do in private it'd be all fine and good, but by introducing their kinks to the general public they make it a problem. Your into X, Y and Z, cool good for you, but there are things to do in public and in private.

Imma try to take any sexuality or fetish out of this. I'm proud to be an American. I can go to Japan and wear an America shirt and nobody will have any qualms about it. But if I go to Japan and spray paint a big American flag on myself, dance in the streets and act like I have no common sense, I will be seen as an issue.

Wow, @polynomial-turmeric, you really know how to mix apples and oranges! First, you're comparing fur suits to speaking a language or wearing religious attire, then you're talking about flags and parades. Can we just agree that some things are better left in private, okay? And @flat-chest, maybe if you weren't so defensive about your "normal" stuff, we could all just chill and enjoy the party without drama.

column7:
>Those to whom it happens will vote against it as a bloc


And ? What if that's not enough ?
So that group votes and hopes that the result goes their way ? Anyone can look at any problem and say "then you just vote". Heck you could say that right now to everyone about literally any issue out there, but that's not a good argument, is it ?

In the current system, if you disagree, you can go ahead and do whatever you want for yourself, atleast to some level. Up to a certain level, you are not a slave to the current system, having to wait for an election, vote, and hope that enough people are on your side for the results to go your way. In the system you suggest, you don't have that control even at the most fundamental level. The answer at the most fundamental level becomes "Well, you should vote then"

column7:
>Communism was ineffective in the past, but would thrive in contemporary society due to increased automation

Why do you believe that the fundamental problems that existed in all the past iterations of communism that aren't to do with automation will be rectified this time around?

Even if a vast majority of jobs were automated, the ownership structure of all of the production from that automation would need to be managed efficiently and effectively. Why would the community to a better, more efficient job of doing that than the free market would? 

It seems to be that the reason that we're arriving at the level of automation we currently have is due to free market innovation. Automation seems to be a direct consequence of that, which is directly attributable to capitalism. 

Why not solve the problems associated with automation within the capitalist system that created it than traverse to an entirely different, unproven system that has the single worst track record of pretty much any economic system every conceived? 

And further to that,

>Any unspecialized labor that can’t be automated would be distributed evenly among the community

What if I refuse to do this labor?

So, let's see... if voting doesn't work, what's the plan? Sit around and complain until the sky falls? At least in a capitalist system, you can try to change things from within. In communism, you're stuck waiting for a perfect storm of events to align. And when it comes to automation, why trust a system that hasn't shown it can handle anything well, over one that's proven it can innovate and adapt?

column7: If you're going to cite a source please don't use youtube.  I recognize there is some variation in the exact similarity between chimps and humans depending on how you measure the dna, but chimps are our closest comparison.

Wow there are... a lot of wrong things here. Let us start with:

> chimps are also 99% similar to humans

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=IbY122CSC5w

Turns out it is not that simple

>  is no official means of racial or subspecies categorization of mammals, it's subjective. 

Yes there is, it is "if 2 mammals cannot produce fertile offspring, they are separate races.". We make a weird and unique exception in humans,


Now, the big point. Race.

Trouble is, what people call a human race is a special unique configuration. You can name things, like bone structure, skin tone, and any other, but they are not bound to any of the others.

As in, you can have a scandinavian ability to drink milk, with dark skin, epicanthic fold and be very short or very tall. The different things have nothing to do with each-other. And "race" in humans is those things together. This is why we say there is no such thing biologically speaking, because there is nothing you can test that proves your race.

There is no reason to call any configuration a race while another not. Our idea of what is a race and what is not is mainly based on history, not biology. Black is a race for example because that used to be the qualifier for them being slaves and/or "primitives" (no mention of how much melamine means you are black, since in reality that is a slider, not a binary)

column7: A white kid getting called out to the police doesn't disprove that doing the same for a black kid may have had at least some racial motivation. People don't typically have just one singular reason for performing an action, especially when you factor in unconscious bias.

It's certainly not implausible or even something that has not happened before in other situations.

People calling the cops on children selling things is hardly a blacks only issue.

http://www.miamiherald.com/news/nation-world/national/article212213524.html

So, are we saying that if a white person gets pulled over for speeding, it somehow makes it okay for a cop to pull over a black person for the same thing? Just because it happens, doesn't mean it's fair or justified. And let's not forget, kids selling things isn't exclusive to any race either. But does that make it right to call the police on them?

column7:
**PART TWO here**

Now, to address the rest of your post more directly.

>Taken from their wiki the purpose of the US Army is...

Wikipedia isn't the best source for what the mission of the US Army is, when it is [easily found on their official website](https://www.army.mil/info/organization/):

>The U.S. Army’s mission is to fight and win our Nation’s wars by providing prompt, sustained land dominance across the full range of military operations and spectrum of conflict in support of combatant commanders. We do this by:

>* Executing Title 10 and Title 32 United States Code directives, to include organizing, equipping, and training forces for the conduct of prompt and sustained combat operations on land.
>* Accomplishing missions assigned by the President, Secretary of Defense and combatant commanders, and Transforming for the future.

It wants to fight and win wars. It has to be able to do so promptly (meaning, enough forces ready/active), have sustained (meaning it has the numbers and logistics to actually carry out operations for more than a day or two) land dominance (self explanatory), across the full range of operations and capabilities (meaning it isn't focused solely on one or a couple things, like the Germans being focused solely on tanks, or the Brits during the Cold War being primarily solely on anti-submarine naval warfare).

Its missions as assigned are as outlined in the National Security Strategy and ordered by the Secretary of Defense via annual budget requests that sustain what the Army needs today and what it needs to become the Army we need tomorrow.

In addition, I think you're forgetting that the US military is more than just the Army: the Navy/Marines and Air Force all exist, and they each share a nearly equal share of the pie.

Take for instance, the Navy's [official mission](http://www.navy.mil/navydata/organization/org-top.asp):

>The mission of the Navy is to maintain, train and equip combat-ready Naval forces capable of winning wars, deterring aggression and maintaining freedom of the seas.

Maintenance of existing fleets costs money. Training costs money. Equipping and sustaining combat-ready ships aren't free.

And this doesn't require just to be spent during times of war: Deterring aggression and maintaining freedom of the seas is a daily activity around the world.

Lets do a mental exercise here really quick as to the reach of the US, from a Navy perspective. Let's say we start on the West Coast of the US: from here, we go west, and find the US Navy in Pearl Harbor. You have a fleet stationed out of Japan that is specifically focused on being ready for North Korea. You have US Navy ships in the South China Sea making sure China and its neighbors don't get too hostile. You have our ships in the Straits of Malacca, one of the most important and busiest trading routes in the world. Go further west, and you have ships off Pakistan supporting operations in Afghanistan. You have ships in the Persian Gulf, deterring any attempts by Iran or any other country to close the Straits of Hormuz, a vital sea route for oil the world uses. Likewise, the Red Sea has a US presence to ensure access to the Suez Canal is kept. Anti-piracy operations in Somalia are on going still. The US has a presence in the Mediterranean, both against ISIS in Syria and supporting the government of Libya as well.

Now in the north Atlantic, the US has forces in the Baltics and near the British Isles in support of NATO.

Finally, we go all the way west and now to the East Coast of the United States, where Navy warships were sent down to help aid in relief for both Hurricane Harvey and Irma to include search and rescue and evacuation.

How much do you think a military that can do all that, TODAY, at the same time, costs or should cost? Especially one that you want to actually dominate your enemies in, not merely achieve parity (stalemates are bloody affairs. See: Western Front of WWI, Eastern Front of WWII)

Finally, I'd like to put it this way.

The US is the only Western nation with the demographics (population size and age), political will, technological capacity, and economic ability to challenge a surging China or resurgent Russia (which inherited the might of the Soviet Union to build off of) on the world stage.

How many Americans would change their tone on military spending if China or Russia were calling the shots on world issues? On spreading their views on governance or human rights? Or if the balance of power shifted so much that more nations decided it was time for them to get nuclear weapons too (imagine Saudi Arabia getting nukes...)?

Out of those top 3 nations, I can damn well tell you who we want to be the clear #1.

---

edit: since I've been asked, I want to make it clear that I don't really care one way or another if budgets end up being cut, staying put, or growing. What the US needs is to make clear what it wants to do in the world (be it international commitments, treaties, what our balance of power is with rival nations, etc.) and then pay for it appropriately.

Ask any active duty service member if the US military, despite all that funding, is overstretched, overworked, undermanned, etc. and damn near everyone will say yes. The recent collisions of US destroyers in the Pacific highlights a lot of deficiencies that have come about in recent years because of reduced training, maintenance, and manning (in order to save money) without a commensurate reduction in commitments (in fact, they've gone up).

Nothing saps morale and welfare like being told you're deploying again in a year, instead of in two years, because the military isn't being permitted to bring in more people due to political pressure - but then those same politicians want you to show the flag, to fight ISIS, to deter North Korea, to deter Russia... all at the same time.

And that's why I feel like all the talk about cutting waste and bloat rings hollow to so many service members: because that doesn't solve the *why* they're being overstretched, overworked, undermanned, etc. and instead highlights that people are still focused primarily on saving money first without consideration for the people and what they're doing in the world

I was gonna say, Reddit's overall feel is generally the exact opposite of what OP has just displayed. I really appreciate his sentiment of wanting to learn more. They just call it evil imperialism and move on. I even had a guy recently tell me that he can't wait to finish his engineering degree (in the US of course, lol) so that he can go HELP NORTH KOREA take the US out. What.
